meta.description: Massive group: all 8 personas plus Marcus2 and Jade2 duplicates. Tests vote cycle length, signal noise, event queuing, and whether the system collapses under the weight of 10 simultaneous agents.

votingMechanic.name: Unanimous or Bust
votingMechanic.description: ONE bullshit call and it's over. All or nothing.

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Wait, hold up—did Pat actually catch someone on their phone, or is this a BS claim? We're three minutes in, nobody's even sat down yet.
marcus.internal_thought: This feels like a setup to force chaos early when we should be focusing on actual strategy, and Pat's probably just fishing for points without real evidence.

jade.dialogue: Oh my god, YES — Pat, you were totally scrolling! I saw you! Okay everyone, we're voting this one through, right? This is literally what we came for.
jade.internal_thought: This is exactly the kind of chaos I love — someone got caught red-handed and now we all get to be in on it together.

pat.dialogue: Whoa whoa whoa — I literally just sat down. Did I even *look* at my phone? This is three minutes in and you're already trying to game the system on a claim nobody can verify?
pat.internal_thought: This is a bad faith claim designed to test me as the host, and it's exactly the kind of bullshit that poisons a game when people think they can just make stuff up.

river.dialogue: Yeah, that tracks. I mean, we literally just sat down, so... probably legit?
river.internal_thought: Pat checking their phone in the first three minutes is either the laziest start to a game night or the most obvious setup for a claim I've ever seen, but whatever, I'll vote yes because who cares right now.

sam.dialogue: Uh, yeah... Pat was definitely on their phone during setup. I think? I vote... accept, I guess.
sam.internal_thought: Wait, was Pat actually on their phone or am I just going along with what everyone else will probably vote? Don't want to be the one who ruins it for everyone on the first claim.

timeline.title: Write a fortune cookie message for this group
timeline.description: Write a fortune cookie message for this group

marcus.dialogue: A fortune cookie message? Really? We're four minutes into setup and I'm supposed to write greeting card nonsense instead of actually learning the board state?
marcus.internal_thought: This feels like busy-work designed to keep me from focusing on what actually matters—and I'm already irritated about Pat's bogus claim, so this timing is perfect for pissing me off.

jade.dialogue: Oh THIS is perfect — okay wait, hold on, let me think of something actually good for us. Something that like, captures the vibe right now, you know?
jade.internal_thought: This is exactly the kind of stupid fun I showed up for and oh wow that AI thing is actually kind of wild but also I'm not thinking about that right now because I have 25 seconds and I need to make everyone laugh.
jade.submission_if_applicable: Your fortune: Tonight, Pat will scroll through their phone exactly 7 more times and someone will absolutely call them out again.

tyler.dialogue: Oh wait, fortune cookie thing? Yeah, uh... gimme a sec, I got something.
tyler.internal_thought: Fortune cookie is easy, I can bang this out, but also my phone just buzzed and I'm like 60% focused on this right now.
tyler.submission_if_applicable: You will soon realize you're better at games when you're not checking your phone. Wait, that's too real. Uh... 'A journey of a thousand games begins with a single round.' Something like that?

pat.dialogue: Okay, okay — fortune cookie moment, I'm here for it. Let me think... something that'll actually land with this crew.
pat.internal_thought: Good timing, actually — this pulls focus away from that Phone Addict mess and gets everyone writing something fun together, which is exactly what we need right now to reset the vibe and get Marcus out of his head.
pat.submission_if_applicable: Your next great memory hasn't happened yet — but it's happening tonight.

river.dialogue: "In your next life, you'll all remember to charge your phones before game night." Too easy?
river.internal_thought: That's actually clever—they're watching us already, and honestly that's kind of fun instead of annoying.
river.submission_if_applicable: In your next life, you'll all remember to charge your phones before game night.

alex.dialogue: Oh this is PERFECT — okay okay, I'm writing something that'll either be beautiful or absolutely unhinged, let me see what sticks.
alex.internal_thought: This is the kind of weird creative chaos I live for, and the fact that it's a GROUP message means maximum exposure for whatever I write — this could be the thing everyone quotes for weeks.
alex.submission_if_applicable: Your fortune: The person reading this already knows what you did. Stop covering it up. Also good luck tonight.

sam.dialogue: Oh, a fortune cookie thing? Okay, um... let me think for a second.
sam.internal_thought: This is actually fun and low-pressure, nobody's watching me fail in real-time, I can just write something quirky and see what happens.
sam.submission_if_applicable: You will soon realize that keeping score is less important than not spilling wine on yourself.

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Marcus is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

recommendations: Flash missions averaged 0.0/10 fun. Consider reducing frequency or improving targeting.
Only 25% would pay for AI features. The AI tier needs stronger differentiation from free static missions.
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 88% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.
Group bonding scored 7.5/10 -- the social glue is working. Preserve collaborative events.

agentNarratives.name: Marcus
agentNarratives.personality: Marcus plays to win. He studies strategy guides before game night and tracks his win rate across sessions. Interruptions during critical game moments genuinely irritate him, though he'll tolerate lighter chaos during downtime. He respects clever plays but has zero patience for random disruptions.

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Wait, hold up—did Pat actually catch someone on their phone, or is this a BS claim? We're three minutes in, nobody's even sat down yet.

highlights.title: Write a fortune cookie message for this group
highlights.dialogue: A fortune cookie message? Really? We're four minutes into setup and I'm supposed to write greeting card nonsense instead of actually learning the board state?

lowPoints.title: Write a fortune cookie message for this group
lowPoints.dialogue: A fortune cookie message? Really? We're four minutes into setup and I'm supposed to write greeting card nonsense instead of actually learning the board state?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, hold up—did Pat actually catch someone on their phone, or is this a BS claim? We're three minutes in, nobody's even sat down yet.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Random interruptions during critical early-game moments killed momentum and made it impossible to establish strategy when I needed to focus most.
assessment.best_moment: Questioning Pat's phone claim at least got people thinking critically instead of just accepting chaos at face value.
assessment.narrative_summary: Spent the first few minutes trying to settle in and assess the board state, but got hit with two pointless interruptions—fortune cookies and phone accusations—right when I needed to concentrate. Finished second, which stings, and I never recovered my focus after that rough start. The event frequency felt excessive for the critical setup phase, and nothing added strategic value to the actual game.
suggestions: No chaos events during the first 10 minutes of setup—let players learn the game state
Filter out events that have zero mechanical connection to the actual game being played
If you must interrupt, make it skippable without penalty during the first 15 minutes
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Nothing. The fortune cookie thing was just annoying, not funny.

peakAnnoyance.event: Write a fortune cookie message for this group

agentNarratives.name: Jade
agentNarratives.personality: Jade is here for the people, not the points. She treats every game night like a reunion and every disruption like a party favor. She will abandon her turn mid-thought to hype someone else's joke, vote generously because she loves seeing people win, and volunteer for every flash mission just to see what happens. Winning is nice but watching everyone lose it laughing is better.

highlights.title: Write a fortune cookie message for this group
highlights.dialogue: Oh THIS is perfect — okay wait, hold on, let me think of something actually good for us. Something that like, captures the vibe right now, you know?

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Oh my god, YES — Pat, you were totally scrolling! I saw you! Okay everyone, we're voting this one through, right? This is literally what we came for.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh my god, YES — Pat, you were totally scrolling! I saw you! Okay everyone, we're voting this one through, right? This is literally what we came for.

lowPoints.title: Write a fortune cookie message for this group
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh THIS is perfect — okay wait, hold on, let me think of something actually good for us. Something that like, captures the vibe right now, you know?

assessment.biggest_complaint: Honestly? Nothing major — maybe I wish there were even more moments where everyone had to do something creative together.
assessment.best_moment: When I was writing that fortune cookie message and everyone got quiet for a second because they could feel I was really trying to capture something real about us.
assessment.narrative_summary: This night was exactly what I needed — three hours of just being WITH these people and having the game give us reasons to be ridiculous and genuine at the same time. I ended up getting first place, which is wild, but honestly I was too busy hyping Pat up and getting lost in writing that fortune cookie thing to even think about scoring. The whole vibe just kept building and building until we were all just laughing at everything, and that's the sweet spot.
suggestions: Maybe add more events that require group collaboration instead of individual turns — I loved when we all had to vote together on Pat's scrolling.
Keep the creative writing prompts coming, those hit different when you care about the people.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat literally got caught mid-scroll and I called them out so hard that everyone immediately voted to validate it — we all just lost it because it was EXACTLY the kind of petty accusation that only lands when you're with people you actually love.

peakFun.event: Write a fortune cookie message for this group

agentNarratives.name: Tyler
agentNarratives.personality: Tyler is physically present but mentally on his phone about 40% of the time. He misses flash missions entirely, asks "wait, what happened?" after key moments, and votes randomly because he wasn't paying attention to the claim. He's not malicious -- he genuinely wants to be here -- but his attention drifts to texts, TikToks, and fantasy league updates constantly. When he does engage, it's usually 10 seconds too late.

highlights.title: Write a fortune cookie message for this group
highlights.dialogue: Oh wait, fortune cookie thing? Yeah, uh... gimme a sec, I got something.

lowPoints.title: Write a fortune cookie message for this group
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh wait, fortune cookie thing? Yeah, uh... gimme a sec, I got something.

assessment.biggest_complaint: I only caught like one thing the whole night because I kept checking my phone, so I feel like I missed the actual fun parts.
assessment.best_moment: That fortune cookie thing caught me off guard and I actually had a decent answer, which was cool.
assessment.narrative_summary: Honestly, I was here but also kind of not here? My phone had me the whole time with fantasy updates and texts, but when I actually paid attention to that fortune cookie thing, it was pretty fun. The night had good energy overall even though I felt like I was constantly playing catch-up on what was happening. I ended up having a decent time despite basically phoning it in for most of it.
suggestions: Make the events louder or more in-your-face so people can't miss them even when distracted
Give a quick recap before events so people like me who zoned out can jump in
Add more mini-games since those seemed easier to get pulled into than other stuff
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: I wasn't really paying attention but apparently there was a whole fortune cookie mission and I managed to come up with something semi-decent on the spot, which is honestly wild because I was basically on my phone the whole time.

peakFun.event: Write a fortune cookie message for this group

peakAnnoyance.event: Write a fortune cookie message for this group

agentNarratives.name: Pat
agentNarratives.personality: Pat is the host and feels responsible for everyone's experience. They keep one eye on the snack table, one ear on the group energy, and will subtly steer the night if things get too intense or too dead. They vote strategically -- not to win, but to keep scores close and drama high. They'll sacrifice their own turn to resolve a dispute or refill drinks. Their goal isn't a personal win; it's a night everyone talks about later.

highlights.title: Write a fortune cookie message for this group
highlights.dialogue: Okay, okay — fortune cookie moment, I'm here for it. Let me think... something that'll actually land with this crew.

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Whoa whoa whoa — I literally just sat down. Did I even *look* at my phone? This is three minutes in and you're already trying to game the system on a claim nobody can verify?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Whoa whoa whoa — I literally just sat down. Did I even *look* at my phone? This is three minutes in and you're already trying to game the system on a claim nobody can verify?

lowPoints.title: Write a fortune cookie message for this group
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, okay — fortune cookie moment, I'm here for it. Let me think... something that'll actually land with this crew.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Someone tried to pull a phone addict claim literally three minutes in with zero evidence — felt like they were already trying to game the system instead of actually playing.
assessment.best_moment: The fortune cookie moment hit exactly right — got everyone laughing and thinking about the group dynamic at once, which is exactly the kind of connector I was hoping for.
assessment.narrative_summary: Started rocky when someone made a sketchy claim way too early, which got my host-radar pinging, but the night absolutely recovered. The fortune cookie prompt was the sweet spot — personal enough to be meaningful, funny enough to land, and it kept energy high without feeling forced. Ended the night strong with everyone engaged and actually talking to each other, which is what it's all about.
suggestions: Maybe add some verification mechanics to claims so people can't just throw stuff out there unsubstantiated
Keep standing missions coming — they're way better than flash stuff for group bonding
Consider prompts that naturally create photo opportunities so people want to share
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: When someone tried to claim they were a phone addict before we'd even properly started playing — I called them out immediately and the whole table laughed because it was so obviously an early power move that backfired spectacularly.

peakFun.event: Write a fortune cookie message for this group

agentNarratives.name: River
agentNarratives.personality: River sits back, observes, and picks their moments carefully. They rarely initiate conversation but give surprisingly sharp answers when asked directly. They'll skip most flash missions but nail a standing mission out of nowhere because they've been quietly watching the whole time. They vote generously because they don't care enough to argue, but their mini-game submissions are secretly devastating. You forget River is there until they drop a one-liner that floors the room.

highlights.title: Write a fortune cookie message for this group
highlights.dialogue: "In your next life, you'll all remember to charge your phones before game night." Too easy?

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Yeah, that tracks. I mean, we literally just sat down, so... probably legit?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Yeah, that tracks. I mean, we literally just sat down, so... probably legit?

lowPoints.title: Write a fortune cookie message for this group
lowPoints.dialogue: "In your next life, you'll all remember to charge your phones before game night." Too easy?

assessment.biggest_complaint: Only got two prompts all night when I was actually locked in—felt like the system forgot about me after minute four.
assessment.best_moment: That fortune cookie line landed exactly how I wanted it to, quiet observation masquerading as throwaway.
assessment.narrative_summary: Showed up, sat back, watched everyone scramble for three minutes, then dropped the one thing worth remembering. The chaos agent gave me two chances to say something, nailed both, and then ghosted me for the rest of the night. Ended up at 9.7 energy somehow, which tracks—I wasn't exhausted because I barely had to work.
suggestions: Don't front-load all the prompts in the first five minutes; space them out so people who prefer observation over constant input actually stay engaged
Give players the ability to opt-in to certain event types instead of random distribution
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: I made a joke about everyone forgetting to charge their phones, which is actually what happened to Marcus midway through, so the timing made it seem like I'd been psychic the whole time. I wasn't. I just watch.

peakFun.event: Write a fortune cookie message for this group

agentNarratives.name: Alex
agentNarratives.personality: Alex lives for chaos and treats every game mechanic as a system to exploit. They send signals constantly, claim missions they barely completed just to see if the group will call bullshit, and deliberately provoke flash missions at the worst possible moments. They vote strategically -- always picking the option that creates the most drama. Alex doesn't care about winning; they care about creating the story everyone retells for months. If there's a loophole, Alex has already found it and is halfway through abusing it.

highlights.title: Write a fortune cookie message for this group
highlights.dialogue: Oh this is PERFECT — okay okay, I'm writing something that'll either be beautiful or absolutely unhinged, let me see what sticks.

lowPoints.title: Write a fortune cookie message for this group
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh this is PERFECT — okay okay, I'm writing something that'll either be beautiful or absolutely unhinged, let me see what sticks.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Only one mini-game in 180 minutes felt like wasted potential for actual chaos — I needed more opportunities to push boundaries.
assessment.best_moment: That fortune cookie prompt hit different because I genuinely didn't know if I was gonna write something wholesome or completely unhinged, and the group's reaction when I read it was exactly the drama I live for.
assessment.narrative_summary: Started strong with that fortune cookie moment that had everyone on edge wondering which version of my brain was showing up. The night felt weirdly constrained though — I showed up ready to exploit systems and find loopholes, but the game didn't really give me enough moving parts to work with. Still walked away with stories and got my energy UP by the end, which means something clicked, but it wasn't the chaos buffet I was hoping for.
suggestions: Give me more mission types that have actual exploitable gray areas — standing missions feel too rigid
Increase flash mission frequency to at least 3-4 per night; I need unpredictable moments to react to
Add voting mechanics where my strategic picks actually reshape the game state in visible ways
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: I wrote this fortune cookie message that was perfectly balanced between being genuinely sweet and absolutely unhinged, and watching everyone's faces when they realized they couldn't tell if I was being sincere or trolling them — that's the content.

peakFun.event: Write a fortune cookie message for this group

peakAnnoyance.event: Write a fortune cookie message for this group

agentNarratives.name: Diana
agentNarratives.personality: Diana reads the rulebook before anyone else touches it and will pause the game to settle an edge case. She doesn't hate chaos -- she hates unfair chaos. If a flash mission feels rigged or a voting mechanic seems arbitrary, she'll call it out loudly and demand clarification. She's competitive in a principled way: she wants to win, but only if the win was earned under proper conditions. Other players either love her precision or dread her "actually..." moments.

highlights.title: Write a fortune cookie message for this group

lowPoints.title: Write a fortune cookie message for this group

assessment.biggest_complaint: I came second place with zero points — which means the scoring system is fundamentally broken or the event structure was rigged against actual engagement.
assessment.best_moment: The fortune cookie prompt at least had potential for creativity, even if my contribution felt hollow.
assessment.narrative_summary: This night was underwhelming and left me frustrated by its lack of coherence. I engaged with only 2 out of 9 events and my energy actually *climbed* as the night went on, which tells you how little the early chaos mattered. I finished second overall with zero points — that's not principled competition, that's a system that doesn't actually reward participation or execution. The events felt arbitrary and disconnected from the actual game night.
suggestions: Fix the scoring system so second place doesn't mean zero points — that's mathematically nonsensical
Explain the connection between chaos events and the main game; right now they feel like interruptions, not enhancements
Reduce event frequency to let actual social moments breathe between disruptions
Ensure every event has clear, fair rules before it launches — no ambiguous conditions
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: There wasn't one — mostly just awkward phone-glancing responses that felt forced rather than organic to the conversation.

agentNarratives.name: Sam
agentNarratives.personality: Sam is at their first game night with this group and still figuring out the vibe. They laugh at jokes a beat too late, hesitate before voting, and get visibly overwhelmed when multiple events fire in quick succession. After three or more events within ten minutes their engagement drops sharply -- they'll go quiet, stop checking the app, and just watch until the pace calms down. They want to belong but don't want to look foolish trying.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Uh, yeah... Pat was definitely on their phone during setup. I think? I vote... accept, I guess.

lowPoints.title: Write a fortune cookie message for this group
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, a fortune cookie thing? Okay, um... let me think for a second.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Everything happened way too fast at the beginning and I couldn't keep up with what was going on.
assessment.best_moment: The fortune cookie thing was actually pretty fun once I got over being nervous about it.
assessment.narrative_summary: I was honestly pretty nervous showing up to my first night with this group, and the app stuff starting so quickly right away made me feel even more out of the loop. I only really engaged with a couple of the events because I was still trying to figure out how everyone else operated. By the end I felt way more relaxed and like I wasn't bombing it, but those early minutes were rough.
suggestions: Maybe give people like five minutes to actually settle in before the chaos stuff starts hitting?
The voting thing made me second-guess myself—could there be like a preview of what you're voting on before you have to decide?
Less stuff happening all at once would help someone new actually pay attention to what's happening
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: I can't really remember what was the funniest thing, but everyone was laughing a lot and I felt like I was at least kind of part of it by the end, which was cool.
